articles.title: Why a Deadly Lung Cancer Keeps Coming Back
articles.summary: Scientists found out why a very dangerous lung cancer called small cell lung cancer often comes back after treatment. This cancer is very aggressive and hard to cure, with only 5 out of 100 people surviving five years. A team led by Professor Dr. Silvia von Karstedt discovered that the cancer cells are missing a protein called caspase-8. Without this protein, the cells die in a way that causes inflammation, which makes the body's immune system weaker and helps the cancer grow and spread. The inflammation also makes the cancer cells act more like nerve cells, which helps them spread faster and come back. This research could help doctors find better treatments and ways to detect the cancer early. The study was done on mice and published in a science journal.

articles.title: New Deep-Sea Creatures Found in the Ocean
articles.summary: Scientists have found 24 new types of tiny animals called amphipods in the deep sea of the Pacific Ocean. This area is called the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, and it is very big and not well understood. The scientists worked together in a special workshop to study these animals. They found one group that is so different it is called a new superfamily, which is very rare. This discovery helps us learn more about life in the deep sea, where many species are still unknown. The project is part of a bigger effort to name 1,000 new species by 2030. By identifying these animals, scientists can protect the ocean better and share knowledge about its amazing creatures.

articles.title: How a Diabetes Medicine Works in the Brain
articles.summary: Metformin is a medicine that helps people with type 2 diabetes by lowering their blood sugar. For over 60 years, doctors have used it, but they didn't know exactly how it worked. Scientists from Baylor College of Medicine and other places have found a new way it works: in the brain. They discovered that metformin affects a small protein called Rap1 in a part of the brain called the hypothalamus. When metformin stops Rap1 from working, it helps control blood sugar. The scientists tested this on mice with diabetes and saw that metformin didn't work if Rap1 was missing. This shows the brain is important for how metformin helps. This discovery could lead to better diabetes treatments in the future.

articles.title: Turning Bourbon Waste into Energy Storage
articles.summary: Scientists in Kentucky found a way to turn waste from making bourbon into something useful. Bourbon is a type of whiskey that creates a lot of leftover grain and watery mash called stillage. This waste is often thrown away or used for animal feed, but it can be messy and costly. Chemists at the University of Kentucky used a special cooking method with heat and pressure to change the stillage into a black powder. They then heated this powder to make two types of carbon: hard carbon and activated carbon. They used the activated carbon to build devices called supercapacitors, which can store energy like batteries. In tests, these supercapacitors stored as much energy as some commercial ones, and a hybrid version stored even more. This discovery could help reduce waste and create new ways to store energy for things like electronics.

articles.title: A Bright Star's Hot Mystery Solved
articles.summary: A star named Gamma Cassiopeiae, which you can see in the sky, has been puzzling scientists for 50 years because it gives off very strong and hot X-rays that are unusual for a star like it. Astronomers used a special telescope called XRISM to look at the star and found out that the X-rays come from a small, dense star called a white dwarf that orbits around it. This white dwarf has a magnetic field that pulls material from the bigger star, creating the hot X-rays. The discovery helps scientists understand a new type of star system and opens up more questions about how stars change over time. The research was done by a team from the University of Liège and published in a science journal.

articles.title: The Big Blackout in Spain and Portugal: What Happened and Why?
articles.summary: In 2025, Spain and Portugal had a big blackout where all the lights went out. A report found that the electrical grid had problems with vibrations called oscillations. These vibrations made some power equipment disconnect too early. The grid operators worked hard to fix it quickly. The report says that rules and policies in Spain made the grid weak and at risk. It suggests changes to prevent future blackouts. Data from power stations and solar panels helped experts understand what went wrong. They learned that managing different types of power, like reactive power, is important to keep the grid stable. This event shows why we need safe and strong electricity systems.
